claudeMd:
# Quantum Runner — Implementation Guide You are building a complete browser-based turn-based grid game called Quantum Runner. The player doesn't directly control a character — instead, each turn generates N stochastic possible futures and the player selects which one to "collapse into" by accepting or passing. ## Tech Stack - **Vite 6** with `vanilla-ts` template — scaffold with `npm create vite@latest . -- --template vanilla-ts` then clean up the default files - **TypeScript** (strict mode) — all game logic in `src/` - **HTML5 Canvas 2D API** — all game rendering on a single `` element - **Plain HTML/CSS** — for Accept/Pass buttons and HUD overlays - **No frameworks** (no React, no Phaser, no game engines) - **Nginx alpine** — for Docker production serving - **JetBrains Mono** font via Google Fonts CDN ## Design Direction: Terminal Phosphor Dark retro-CRT aesthetic. NOT generic web-app styling. - Background: `#0a0a0f` (near-black) - Grid lines: `#1a3a2a` (subtle dark green) - Protagonist: `#00ffcc` (bright cyan) — rendered as a diamond/chevron shape - Enemy: `#ff6b35` (amber-orange) — rendered as a jagged/spiky shape - Goal: `#39ff14` (neon green) — rendered as a pulsing circle/beacon - Danger/dead: `#ff1744` (red) - Future overlay: 40% opacity entity colors + dashed borders + directional arrows - UI text: `#b0ffb0` (soft phosphor green) - Accent: `#ffeb3b` (yellow for highlights/selected items) - Font: JetBrains Mono everywhere (load from Google Fonts) - Optional: subtle CSS scanline overlay on the canvas container for atmosphere ## Architecture Build in this order: ### 1. Project Setup - Scaffold Vite vanilla-ts project in the repo root - Configure `tsconfig.json` with strict mode - Clean out default Vite template files (counter.ts, etc.) - Set up `index.html` with canvas element + UI container - Set up `src/style.css` with the dark theme, font import, layout ### 2. Core Types & Config (`src/types.ts`, `src/config.ts`) ```typescript // types.ts export type CellType = 'floor' | 'wall' | 'goal'; export interface Position { x: number; y: number; } export interface Entity { type: 'player' | 'enemy'; id: string; pos: Position; } export interface GameState { grid: CellType[][]; player: Entity; enemies: Entity[]; goalPos: Position; turn: number; status: 'playing' | 'won' | 'lost'; } export interface Future { state: GameState; description: string; quality: number; // -1 to 1 } export type GamePhase = 'title' | 'playing' | 'choosing' | 'won' | 'lost'; ``` ```typescript // config.ts export const CONFIG = { GRID_COLS: 12, GRID_ROWS: 10, CELL_SIZE: 56, NUM_FUTURES: 3, // default futures per turn (2-5) MIN_FUTURES: 2, MAX_FUTURES: 5, ENEMY_CHASE_BIAS: 0.6, // probability enemy moves toward player PLAYER_GOAL_BIAS: 0.3, // slight bias toward goal in movement sampling } as const; ``` ### 3. Grid & Level (`src/state/Grid.ts`, `src/state/level.ts`) - `Grid` class: wraps the 2D CellType array, provides `isWalkable(x,y)`, `getNeighbors(x,y)`, `manhattanDistance(a,b)` - `level.ts`: hardcoded level definition — a 12×10 grid with walls forming corridors. Player starts near (1,8), goal at (10,1). Two enemies at ~(5,4) and (8,2). Multiple viable paths. Design walls to create interesting corridor choices. Level layout concept (W=wall, .=floor, G=goal, P=player start, E=enemy start): ``` . . W . . . . . . . G . . . W . . . . . E . . . . . W . . W W . . . . . . . . . . W . . . W W . . . . . E . . . . . . . . W W . . . W W . . . . . . . . . . . . . W . . . . W W W . . . . W . . . P . . . . W . . . . . . . . . . . W . . . . . ``` Adjust to ensure playability — multiple paths from P to G, enemies positioned to create tension. ### 4. Futures Engine (`src/engine/FuturesEngine.ts`, `src/engine/movement.ts`) **movement.ts**: - `samplePlayerMove(state: GameState): Position` — returns a random adjacent walkable cell, biased toward the goal via softmax over negative Manhattan distance. Include "stay in place" as an option with low weight. - `sampleEnemyMove(enemy: Entity, state: GameState): Position` — returns a random adjacent walkable cell, biased toward the player (ENEMY_CHASE_BIAS chance of moving in the best direction, otherwise random). Include "stay" option. **FuturesEngine.ts**: - `generateFutures(state: GameState, n: number): Future[]` — generates n futures by: 1. For each future: sample a player move, sample each enemy move, create new GameState 2. Check collisions: player on enemy cell → lost, player on goal → won 3. Compute quality score: based on (new player-goal distance vs old) and (new enemy-player distances vs old) 4. Generate description string: "You drift north. Enemy Alpha closes in." etc. 5. **Ensure variance**: if all futures have similar quality (spread < threshold), regenerate some with adjusted probabilities. At minimum, ensure at least one future moves player closer to goal and one has an enemy moving closer. ### 5. Renderer (`src/render/`) **Renderer.ts**: Main class that holds the canvas context and coordinates sub-renderers. - `render(state: GameState, futureOverlay?: Future)` — clear canvas, draw grid, draw entities, optionally draw future overlay **GridRenderer.ts**: - Draw cell backgrounds (dark for floor, lighter block pattern for walls) - Draw subtle grid lines in `#1a3a2a` - Highlight the goal cell with a pulsing glow effect **EntityRenderer.ts**: - Player: cyan diamond shape with slight glow - Enemy: amber-orange spiky/angular shape - Goal: green pulsing beacon/ring - Use geometric shapes, not sprites — keep it clean and readable **FutureOverlay.ts**: - When a future is being previewed: draw translucent (40% opacity) versions of all entities at their FUTURE positions - Draw dashed lines from current position to future position for player and each enemy - Color-code the arrows: green if entity moves toward goal (player) or away from player (enemy = good), red/amber if dangerous - Draw a subtle highlight on the cells that change ### 6. UI & Game Controller (`src/ui/`) **GameController.ts**: The central state machine. - Manages `GamePhase`: title → playing → choosing → won/lost - On 'playing': calls FuturesEngine to generate futures, transitions to 'choosing' - On 'choosing': TurnManager handles the streaming UI - On accept: applies the chosen future, checks win/lose, loops back to 'playing' or transitions to 'won'/'lost' **TurnManager.ts**: Handles the future-selection UX. - Holds the array of futures and current index - `showNextFuture()`: advances to next future, triggers overlay render - `acceptCurrent()`: returns the current future, resets - `passCurrent()`: if more futures, advance; if last future, auto-accept - Exposes `currentFuture`, `futuresRemaining`, `isLastFuture` for UI **HUD.ts**: Canvas-rendered heads-up display. - Turn counter (top-left) - "Future X of N" indicator (top-right) - Current future description text (bottom) - Accept/Pass button labels (drawn or HTML overlaid) **screens.ts**: Full-canvas screens for title, win, lose. - Title: "QUANTUM RUNNER" in large JetBrains Mono, "Press Enter to Start", brief rules summary - Win: "REALITY COLLAPSED — YOU WIN" + turn count + restart prompt - Lose: "TIMELINE TERMINATED" + what happened + restart prompt ### 7. Main Entry Point (`src/main.ts`) - Get canvas element, set size - Initialize GameController, Renderer - Wire up keyboard events (Enter=accept/start, Space=pass, R=restart) - Wire up click events for Accept/Pass buttons - Start on title screen ### 8. HTML & CSS (`index.html`, `src/style.css`) **index.html**: ```html
``` **style.css**: Dark background, centered game container, styled buttons matching the phosphor theme. Buttons should have a terminal/retro look — bordered, monospace text, glow on hover. ### 9. Dockerfile Multi-stage build: ```dockerfile FROM node:22-alpine AS build WORKDIR /app COPY package*.json ./ RUN npm ci COPY . . RUN npm run build FROM nginx:alpine COPY --from=build /app/dist /usr/share/nginx/html EXPOSE 80 CMD ["nginx", "-g", "daemon off;"] ``` ## Key Implementation Notes 1. **Immutable states**: Always create new GameState objects for futures. Use spread/structuredClone. Never mutate the current state. 2. **Deep clone for futures**: `JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(state))` or `structuredClone()` for creating future state copies. Prefer `structuredClone`. 3. **Responsive canvas**: Set canvas width/height based on grid dimensions × cell size. Don't try to be responsive — fixed size is fine for a prototype. Center it in the viewport. 4. **Event-driven rendering**: No `requestAnimationFrame` loop. Render on state change: when a new future is shown, when a future is accepted, when screens change. Only use rAF for the goal pulsing animation (a single ongoing animation). 5. **Button state management**: Disable Accept/Pass buttons when not in 'choosing' phase. Hide them on title/win/lose screens. Show keyboard hints. 6. **Quality spread algorithm**: When generating futures, compute the quality of each. If max-min quality spread < 0.3, discard the worst future and regenerate with biased probabilities. Cap retries at 3 to avoid infinite loops. 7. **Description generation**: Keep it terse and atmospheric. "You phase north. Sentinel Alpha advances." "Drift west into the corridor. All clear." "Hold position. Sentinel Beta flanks east." 8. **Test the game is winnable**: With 3 futures per turn and the level layout above, the player should be able to win in ~15-25 turns by choosing good futures. If futures are too random, increase PLAYER_GOAL_BIAS. ## Gotchas - Vite's `vanilla-ts` template includes a `counter.ts` and default `main.ts` + `style.css` — delete/replace these entirely - Canvas `font` must be set AFTER the font is loaded. Use `document.fonts.ready` before first render, or set a fallback. - Canvas text rendering: use `ctx.textAlign` and `ctx.textBaseline` for centering - `structuredClone` doesn't work with functions — game state should be plain data only - Nginx in Docker needs `daemon off;` in CMD or it exits immediately - The Vite build output goes to `dist/` by default — that's what Nginx should serve - Make sure `package.json` has `"type": "module"` (Vite default) ## Acceptance Criteria Mapping 1. Grid-based level with protagonist, goal, enemy → Level layout + EntityRenderer 2. N configurable futures (2-5) → CONFIG.NUM_FUTURES + FuturesEngine 3. Streaming future presentation (Accept/Pass) → TurnManager 4. Passed futures not revisitable, last auto-applied → TurnManager logic 5. Visual future overlay → FutureOverlay renderer 6. Win/lose conditions → GameState.status + collision checks 7. Meaningful variance in futures → Quality spread algorithm 8. Restart after win/loss → GameController state machine 9. Responsive core loop → Event-driven render + keyboard/click controls

checks: A dark-themed grid-based game level is visible in the browser with a retro terminal/phosphor aesthetic — cyan diamond (player), amber-orange shapes (enemies), and a green pulsing beacon (goal) are all rendered on the grid
A title screen displays 'QUANTUM RUNNER' with a start prompt before gameplay begins
During gameplay, a future overlay appears showing translucent/ghost versions of entities at proposed positions with dashed lines indicating movement directions
Two buttons labeled 'ACCEPT' and 'PASS' are visible and functional during the future-choosing phase — clicking Accept applies the shown future, clicking Pass advances to the next
A 'Future X of N' indicator shows which future is currently being previewed and how many remain
When all futures are passed without accepting, the last future is automatically applied (the game advances without requiring explicit accept on the final option)
When the player entity reaches the goal cell through accepted futures, a win screen appears with a restart option
When an enemy entity occupies the same cell as the player in an accepted future, a lose screen appears with a restart option
Pressing Enter/Space on win or lose screens (or clicking restart) returns to the title screen and a new game can be started
Different futures presented each turn show meaningfully different outcomes — some move the player closer to the goal while others are more dangerous, creating actual strategic choice
